evaluation_inputs.system_description: A large-scale, complex software system currently in Spiral 1 development, intended for significant future enhancement including Net-Ready Key Performance Parameters (NR-KPP). The system utilizes a hybrid architecture combining new N-tier structures with substantial legacy code, documented primarily through hierarchical UML and Requisite Pro artifacts.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Standard contract documents (CONOPS, SRS, SSDD) were available in paper/electronic document format. However, native electronic design data (Rational Rose, Requisite Pro) was not accessible, making the hierarchical architecture difficult to browse.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What are the driving architectural constraints, and where are they documented?
How do components communicate and synchronize?
What are the styles of architectural approaches?

hardware_view_description: System must support portability across Linux and Windows NT platforms
Low-level I/O interface layer defined in N-tier architecture to handle platform differences
runtime_constraints: System has a statically set limit for the number of simultaneous connections (links)
Legacy code imposes single-threaded control models on many components

questions: What is the process/thread model of the architecture?
What are the system states and modes?
What variability points are included in the architecture?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Introduction of a Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) for data sharing and enterprise services.
System needs to support a number of links beyond the currently anticipated statically set limit.
Integration of Web Service (WS) based modules across system components.
Replacement of a current component with a known external interface.

business_goals: Achieve Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) compliance
Establish an Open Software Architecture design with layered interfaces
Create an integration model for legacy code, new code, and COTS products
technical_constraints: Limited access to electronic CASE tool data
Short timeline and limited resources for assessment (necessitating a hybrid ATAM)

architectural_approaches.description: A layered architecture separating presentation, business logic, and data access tiers. New components are built using this pattern to establish an Open Software Architecture with clear interfaces.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Clear separation of concerns between layers promotes independent development and maintenance.
Layered interfaces allow new features to be added within or as new layers without disrupting existing functionality.

architectural_approaches.description: Legacy code is integrated using a low-level I/O interface layer (acting as an adapter) to abstract platform differences and provide a consistent API to the new N-tier components.

addressed_attributes.rationale: The adapter layer translates between legacy system interfaces and the new architecture's interfaces, enabling communication.
Abstracts platform-specific details (Linux/Windows NT), allowing components to be more platform-agnostic.

architectural_approaches.description: A combined model integrating legacy code, new N-tier code, and Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) products through defined interfaces and integration patterns.

addressed_attributes.rationale: The model supports mixing different technology stacks and components, allowing for phased modernization and COTS incorporation.
Components are integrated via interfaces, maintaining separation and reducing coupling.

architectural_approaches.description: Use of hierarchical, layered interfaces (as documented in UML) to define clear contracts between system components, supporting the Open Software Architecture goal.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standardized interfaces ensure components can interact predictably.
New components can implement existing interfaces or new interfaces can be added at appropriate layers.

children.refinement: Clear separation of concerns between architectural layers
children.scenario_description: A new business rule needs to be added to the system. The change is isolated to the business logic layer without affecting presentation or data access layers.

children.refinement: Ability to add new features via layered interfaces
children.scenario_description: A new reporting module must be integrated into the system within one development cycle.

children.refinement: Legacy and new components communicate via adapter interfaces
children.scenario_description: A legacy subsystem on Windows NT must exchange data with a new component on Linux.
The system must be migrated from a Linux-based server to a Windows Server environment with minimal code changes.

children.refinement: Support mixing legacy, new, and COTS components
children.scenario_description: A Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) database must replace the existing legacy data store.

scenarios.scenario_text: A new business rule needs to be added to the system. The change is isolated to the business logic layer without affecting presentation or data access layers.
A new reporting module must be integrated into the system within one development cycle.
A legacy subsystem on Windows NT must exchange data with a new component on Linux.
The system must be migrated from a Linux-based server to a Windows Server environment with minimal code changes.
A Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) database must replace the existing legacy data store.

scenario_reference.text: A new business rule needs to be added to the system. The change is isolated to the business logic layer without affecting presentation or data access layers.

analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to add a new business rule
analysis_records.response_measure: Change is contained within the business logic layer; zero changes required in presentation or data access layers.
architectural_decisions: N-Tier Architecture with clear layer separation
analysis_records.reasoning: The N-tier pattern enforces separation of concerns. The business logic layer is a distinct module with defined interfaces. As long as the new rule conforms to the layer's interface contracts and does not violate encapsulation, changes should be isolated.

risks.description: Business rule change inadvertently creates dependencies on data schema or presentation logic, breaking layer isolation.
risks.rationale: Poorly defined interfaces or developers bypassing architectural boundaries could lead to hidden coupling.

non_risks.description: The architectural pattern itself provides a clear framework for modular change.
non_risks.rationale: The N-tier style and layered interfaces are explicitly designed to support this type of isolated modification.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Strictness of interface contracts between layers

conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative effect due to potential inter-layer communication overhead)

scenario_reference.text: A new reporting module must be integrated into the system within one development cycle.

analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to integrate a new reporting module
analysis_records.response_measure: Module integrated and operational within one development cycle (e.g., 3 months).
analysis_records.reasoning: Layered interfaces define integration points. The new module can be added as a new component within the architecture, likely at the presentation or business logic layer, implementing or consuming existing interfaces. The hybrid model supports integrating diverse components.

risks.description: New reporting module has high data access requirements that conflict with existing data layer interfaces or performance constraints.
risks.rationale: If interfaces are not designed for extensibility (e.g., too restrictive), integrating a new module may require significant refactoring, exceeding the timebox.

non_risks.description: The architectural goal of Open Software Architecture via layered interfaces directly supports this scenario.
non_risks.rationale: The documented approach is to use layered interfaces to enable new component integration.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Completeness and stability of the layered interface definitions

conflicting_attributes: Extensibility (positive effect - easier to integrate new components)
Complexity (negative effect - more abstract, harder to implement)

scenario_reference.text: A legacy subsystem on Windows NT must exchange data with a new component on Linux.

analysis_records.stimulus: Data exchange request between legacy (Windows NT) and new (Linux) components
analysis_records.response_measure: Successful, reliable data exchange meeting functional and performance requirements.
analysis_records.reasoning: The adapter pattern abstracts platform-specific details. The legacy subsystem's interface is wrapped by an adapter that provides a standardized API. The new Linux component communicates with this adapter, not directly with the legacy system, mitigating platform differences.

risks.description: Adapter introduces a single point of failure and a performance bottleneck for all cross-platform communication.
risks.rationale: All data must pass through the translation layer. If the adapter is poorly designed or lacks robustness, it can degrade reliability and latency.

risks.description: Legacy subsystem may have undocumented behaviors or data formats that the adapter fails to handle correctly.
risks.rationale: This can lead to data corruption or system errors, compromising interoperability.

non_risks.description: The pattern choice is appropriate for the problem.
non_risks.rationale: Adapter/Wrapper is a standard pattern for integrating legacy systems with different interfaces or platforms.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Adapter's translation logic correctness and completeness
Network latency and reliability between the adapter and the legacy subsystem

conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative effect - adds indirection and processing overhead)

scenario_reference.text: The system must be migrated from a Linux-based server to a Windows Server environment with minimal code changes.

analysis_records.stimulus: Decision to change the host operating system platform
analysis_records.response_measure: Successful migration with minimal code changes (e.g., < 5% of codebase modified).
analysis_records.reasoning: The adapter abstracts platform-specific details of legacy components. Layered interfaces for new components should be defined in a platform-agnostic way. The business logic and presentation layers should rely on these abstractions, not direct OS calls.

risks.description: New N-tier components may have hidden dependencies on Linux-specific libraries or system calls.
risks.rationale: Despite the architectural intent, implementation shortcuts can violate portability assumptions, leading to significant rework during migration.

non_risks.description: The adapter pattern successfully abstracts the legacy platform specifics.
non_risks.rationale: The adapter's purpose is to provide a consistent interface regardless of the underlying legacy OS (Windows NT).

sensitivity_points.parameter: Strictness of using platform-agnostic APIs and libraries in new component development

conflicting_attributes: Performance/Optimization (negative effect - may preclude use of platform-specific optimizations)

scenario_reference.text: A Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) database must replace the existing legacy data store.

analysis_records.stimulus: Decision to replace the legacy data store with a COTS database product
analysis_records.response_measure: Successful replacement with no loss of functionality and within acceptable downtime/cost.
architectural_decisions: N-Tier Architecture (specifically the data access layer)
analysis_records.reasoning: The hybrid model supports mixing components like COTS. In an N-tier architecture, the data access layer is responsible for database interaction. Replacing the data store should primarily impact this layer if it encapsulates database-specific logic behind an interface.

risks.description: Legacy data schema or transaction semantics are incompatible with the COTS database, requiring extensive data migration and business logic changes.
risks.rationale: Flexibility at the architectural layer may be undermined by semantic incompatibilities at the data model layer.

risks.description: The existing data access layer may be tightly coupled to the legacy database's proprietary API, making replacement costly.
risks.rationale: If the abstraction is leaky, the change propagates beyond the data access layer, reducing flexibility.

non_risks.description: The architectural model explicitly accommodates COTS products.
non_risks.rationale: The Hybrid Integration Model is defined to support integrating legacy, new, and COTS components.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Quality of abstraction in the data access layer interface
Complexity of data migration and transformation requirements

tradeoff_points.parameter: Use of a generic data access interface vs. database-specific optimizations
conflicting_attributes: Flexibility/Portability (positive effect - easier to swap databases)
Performance (negative effect - may not leverage advanced DB features)
